quotes.verbatim_quote:
The term “choro” was generically used to describe a small group (flute, cavaquinho
and seven stringed guitar), often with one group member as a soloist (improvisor).
Actually it is also played with other instrumentation (clarinet, trombone and a
few percussion instruments — particularly “pandeiro” and “surdo”). ,
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the canonical choro ensemble and names the specific instruments a guitarist must understand when situating their role in the style.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Most choro melodies are based on 16th notes with frequent use of melody
embellishments. It’s usually written in 2/4, but you will also find it in 3/4 (choro
waltz).
quotes.why_load_bearing: Gives the two defining rhythmic and metric rules a player must know to recognize and perform authentic choro phrasing.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
In the harmony the use of triads, seventh and diminished chords are common,
usually in an inverted position. The bass line is also very important.
quotes.why_load_bearing: States the harmonic vocabulary and the structural priority of inverted voicings and bass movement that distinguish choro accompaniment from other styles.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The choro form is frequently divided in 3 parts — A, B and C — with a modulation

to the V or IV chord key center in the part C.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the standard three-part form and the characteristic modulation rule that a guitarist must follow to navigate choro repertoire correctly.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Like other Brazilian styles, the patterns that we use on the guitar for the choro are
simulations of the rhythm section. This basic pattern is a typical “pandeiro”
pattern applied to the guitar.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Establishes that choro guitar rhythm is a percussive simulation of the pandeiro, which governs how all subsequent patterns are understood.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
You can practice these patterns and variations muting the strings with your left
hand. It’s also a good idea to practice away from the guitar, clapping hands in the
high voices and tapping the low voices with your foot.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Provides a concrete two-part practice method—left-hand muting and body-percussion subdivision—for internalizing the rhythmic independence between high and low voices.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Each variation can be played through the entire song or you can switch between
the variations to create a new pattern. It’s also common to use a different pattern
or add bass line movements at the end of a section or a “cadenza.”
quotes.why_load_bearing: Gives the practical rule for deploying multiple rhythmic variations within a single performance, including the convention of changing patterns at section endings.

quotes.verbatim_quote: You can use this pattern through the entire song or at the end of a section.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Specifies the correct musical context for Variation #5, teaching when this particular pattern is idiomatic.
